title: Why Some Plants Make Poisonous Compounds

lesson.learn: Many plants make poisonous compounds as a defense against being eaten. These chemicals can interfere with nerves, digestion, or cell function, making the plant much less attractive to herbivores. The compounds can be stored in leaves, seeds, bark, or sap, and some are only released when tissue is damaged. That makes the plant dangerous at the moment of feeding, which is exactly when defense matters most. Poison is not a plant flaw. It is an evolutionary strategy that helps a species survive long enough to grow, flower, and reproduce.
lesson.simpler: Some plants make poisons to protect themselves from animals that want to eat them.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Poisonous compounds help plants survive by discouraging herbivores and protecting valuable tissues and seeds.

learnImageCredit.creator: Robert E. Wright
learnImageCredit.license: GNU Free Documentation License, Version 1.2 or later

lesson.deeperDive:
Many of the same chemicals that make plants dangerous also make them medically interesting. A compound can be toxic in one context and useful in another if the dose and delivery are controlled.

That is why plant chemistry has mattered to medicine, ecology, and agriculture for centuries. The line between poison and medicine often comes down to context, dosage, and purification.
lesson.funFact: Some of the most famous medicines in history began as plant toxins before scientists learned how to use them safely.

concepts: Secondary metabolite - Non-growth chemical with ecological roles
Chemical defense - Toxic or unpleasant compound used for protection
